title: A Seminar on the Ethics of Whistleblowing

body.speaker: Professor
body.text: Let's take a case that I think puts pressure on the standard liberal defence of whistleblowing. An employee at a defence contractor discovers evidence that their employer is systematically falsifying safety reports on a weapons system sold to governments. They disclose this to a journalist rather than to an official regulator. Is that whistleblowing in the morally admirable sense, or is it something more complicated?

body.speaker: Student
body.text: I'd say it's still admirable in principle, even if the channel chosen is debatable. The wrongdoing is clear — falsifying safety reports in that context could cost lives. The internal and regulatory routes may be compromised or slow, and there's a public interest in knowing. The journalist route seems to satisfy the conditions most defences of whistleblowing require: serious harm, exhaustion of internal channels, disclosure proportionate to the wrongdoing.

body.speaker: Professor
body.text: Let me push on that. The 'proportionality' condition is doing a lot of work in your account. In this case, the disclosure doesn't just reveal the falsification — it potentially exposes classified technical specifications for the weapons system. Some of that information, in the wrong hands, could itself cause serious harm. Does the whistleblower's good intention mitigate moral responsibility for those secondary consequences?

body.speaker: Student
body.text: That's where I find myself genuinely uncertain. There's a consequentialist line of argument that says the whistleblower bears some moral responsibility for reasonably foreseeable consequences, even if unintended. But there's a competing view — I think it's roughly Kantian — that the moral worth of the action is assessed at the point of decision, not retrospectively in light of consequences that the agent couldn't fully control or predict.

body.speaker: Professor
body.text: The Kantian framing is interesting here, but I think it has a difficulty. The categorical imperative, in the formula relevant to this case, would ask something like: could we universalise a maxim according to which anyone who discovers institutional wrongdoing may unilaterally disclose it through any channel they choose? The problem is that universalising such a maxim would seem to collapse the distinction between legitimate whistleblowing and simple data leakage — including disclosures that are motivated by self-interest, ideological agenda, or factual error.

body.speaker: Student
body.text: That's a real problem, and I think it's why most sophisticated defences of whistleblowing try to build in procedural conditions — not just substantive ones about the severity of the wrongdoing, but conditions about the agent's epistemic position, their sincerity, and the proportionality of the disclosure. The difficulty is that those conditions are much easier to apply retrospectively than they are to specify in advance in a way that provides reliable guidance for someone actually faced with the decision.

body.speaker: Professor
body.text: Exactly. And notice what that concession implies: if the conditions can only be reliably assessed after the fact, then the whistleblower is making a judgment under uncertainty — a judgment that, if wrong, may be catastrophic and irreversible. That structure is familiar from the ethics of civil disobedience, which faces similar problems. The person who breaks the law in the name of justice has to accept, I think, that they are claiming a moral authority that democratic procedures are specifically designed to distribute more broadly. Whether that claim is ever justified, and under what conditions, is one of the hardest questions in political philosophy.

body.speaker: Student
body.text: I want to bring in a consideration that I think tends to be underweighted in these discussions: the structural asymmetry between the whistleblower and the institution. The whistleblower is typically a single individual operating against an organisation with vastly greater legal, financial, and reputational resources. The legal protections for whistleblowers — even in relatively strong jurisdictions — are porous and often litigated away. To hold the whistleblower to a high standard of procedural rectitude while the institution faces no comparable standard of transparency seems to place the entire burden of managing institutional wrongdoing on the most exposed party.

body.speaker: Professor
body.text: That asymmetry argument is important, and I think it's under-theorised in the mainstream literature. But let me push back on one aspect of it. The argument, taken to its logical conclusion, might seem to justify substantially lower procedural standards for any actor who faces a structurally weaker position — which is a principle that could be claimed by a very wide range of actors, including some whose disclosures we would be uncomfortable defending. How do you avoid that conclusion?

body.speaker: Student
body.text: I think you'd have to combine the asymmetry argument with substantive conditions — not use it as a standalone justification for any disclosure. The power asymmetry reduces but doesn't eliminate the requirement for epistemic care, sincerity, and proportionality. And one further condition I'd want to add is that the wrongdoing must be the kind that the institution itself, by its own stated values, would be required to acknowledge — not just something the whistleblower disagrees with, but something that constitutes a genuine betrayal of the institution's claimed purposes. That seems to me to give the asymmetry argument more traction without making it infinitely expandable.

annotations.meaningVi: challenges; identifies a weakness or difficulty in an argument
a company that supplies goods or services — typically weapons or security equipment — to governments or military organisations
the requirement, in most ethical defences of whistleblowing, that the whistleblower first attempt to report wrongdoing through internal mechanisms before going public
carrying significant argumentative weight; being responsible for a key step in the reasoning
reduce but not eliminate the degree to which someone can be held accountable for an outcome
relating to consequentialism — the ethical view that the moral worth of an action is determined entirely by its consequences
relating to the ethical philosophy of Immanuel Kant, which holds that the moral worth of an action is determined by the principle (maxim) guiding it, not its consequences
looking back at past events; evaluated after the fact
Kant's central ethical principle: act only according to a maxim that you could will to become a universal law
to test an action's moral legitimacy by asking whether it would be acceptable if everyone in similar circumstances acted the same way
unauthorised disclosure of information, without the ethical justification typically required of whistleblowing
requirements about how something should be done — the process followed — as distinct from substantive conditions about what is done
the state of one's knowledge — how well-informed, reliable, and justified one's beliefs are
the deliberate, non-violent breaking of a law on moral grounds, typically to draw attention to injustice
the claimed right to make ethical judgments and act on them in ways that override institutional norms or law
an imbalance built into the relationship between two parties — here, the vast difference in power and resources between an individual whistleblower and a large organisation
full of gaps or weaknesses; not reliably preventing harm — here, legal protections that can be circumvented
defeated through legal proceedings — the institution uses the courts to undermine or exhaust the whistleblower's legal protections
strict adherence to correct procedures and ethical process
careful attention to the quality and reliability of one's knowledge before acting

annotations.phrase: betrayal of the institution's claimed purposes
annotations.meaningVi: acting in a way that fundamentally contradicts the stated values and mission of an organisation
grip; persuasive force — the degree to which an argument gains purchase in a debate

grammarNotes.title: Modal hedging for epistemic tentativeness
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc ngữ pháp này xuất hiện nhiều lần trong bài và là đặc trưng của tiếng Anh học thuật trình độ cao. Học viên nên chú ý cách cấu trúc này tổ chức thông tin và tạo nhấn mạnh trong văn phong trang trọng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Both speakers use modal verbs and hedging phrases to signal degrees of certainty: 'I think it's roughly Kantian,' 'might seem to justify,' 'seems to me to give.' This is not vagueness but precision — it signals that the speaker is making a claim while acknowledging it as contestable. The use of 'seems,' 'I think,' and 'roughly' marks the difference between confident assertion and careful intellectual engagement.
examples: There's a competing view — I think it's roughly Kantian — that the moral worth of the action is assessed at the point of decision.
The argument, taken to its logical conclusion, might seem to justify substantially lower procedural standards.

grammarNotes.title: Concessive infinitive: 'to hold X while Y seems to Z'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đây là cấu trúc ngữ pháp phổ biến trong tiếng Anh học thuật C1-C2. Việc nắm vững cấu trúc này giúp học viên đọc và viết văn bản học thuật chuyên sâu một cách tự nhiên và chính xác hơn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'To hold the whistleblower to a high standard of procedural rectitude while the institution faces no comparable standard of transparency seems to place the entire burden of managing institutional wrongdoing on the most exposed party.' The infinitive subject ('To hold...') and the participle ('while... facing') create a complex sentence that balances two conditions. The infinitive subject is a formal alternative to a gerund ('Holding...') and is more characteristic of written or formal spoken register.
examples: To hold the whistleblower to a high standard of procedural rectitude while the institution faces no comparable standard of transparency seems to place the entire burden of managing institutional wrongdoing on the most exposed party.

questions.prompt: What makes the professor's case study a challenge for the standard defence of whistleblowing?
options: The employee discloses information that is not actually true
The employee has a financial motive for the disclosure
The wrongdoing involves personal misconduct rather than institutional policy
The disclosure goes to a journalist rather than an official regulator and may expose classified information

questions.prompt: The student says the journalist route satisfies the standard conditions for admirable whistleblowing. Which conditions does the student cite?
options: Serious harm, exhaustion of internal channels, and proportionate disclosure
Legal compliance, media independence, and public interest
Urgency, specificity, and documented evidence
Anonymity, reversibility, and systemic significance

questions.prompt: What complicating factor does the professor introduce about the disclosure?
options: The employee did not follow internal reporting procedures
The disclosure may expose classified weapons specifications that could themselves cause harm
The journalist may not publish the information responsibly
The falsification of safety reports may not actually be illegal

questions.prompt: The student identifies two ethical frameworks. What is the consequentialist position on the whistleblower's moral responsibility?
options: The whistleblower has no responsibility for unintended consequences
The whistleblower's responsibility ends once disclosure is made
The whistleblower bears some responsibility for reasonably foreseeable consequences, even if unintended
Responsibility is shared equally between the whistleblower and the journalist

questions.prompt: The Kantian view the student describes holds that moral worth is assessed
options: by the net effect on public welfare
by the degree of suffering caused to the institution
by the sincerity of the agent's intention measured against outcomes
at the point of decision, not retrospectively in light of consequences

questions.prompt: The professor applies the categorical imperative and finds that universalising the whistleblower's maxim would
options: collapse the distinction between legitimate whistleblowing and self-interested or erroneous data leakage
require all employees to report wrongdoing to journalists rather than regulators
make all institutional secrecy morally impermissible
justify any disclosure on national security grounds

questions.prompt: The student says sophisticated defences of whistleblowing include procedural conditions. What difficulty does the student identify with these conditions?
options: They are too lenient and allow too many disclosures
They can only be reliably applied retrospectively, not as advance guidance
They conflict with the legal protections available to whistleblowers
They are not recognised in most national legal systems

questions.prompt: The professor compares whistleblowing to civil disobedience because both involve
options: breaking the law as a form of political protest
accepting prison sentences as part of the moral act
claiming a moral authority that democratic procedures are designed to distribute more broadly
publishing information that governments want to keep secret

questions.prompt: The student introduces the 'structural asymmetry' argument. What does this argument highlight?
options: The gap between whistleblower protections in different legal systems
The asymmetry between the harm of wrongdoing and the harm of disclosure
The imbalance between public and private sector whistleblowing rates
The vast difference in legal, financial, and reputational power between individual whistleblowers and institutions

questions.prompt: The student describes whistleblower legal protections as 'porous and often litigated away.' This means
options: protections exist but have gaps and are frequently defeated by institutional litigation
the laws are poorly written and confusing to apply
whistleblowers rarely receive legal advice before disclosing
legal protection is only available after wrongdoing has been proven

questions.prompt: The professor's counter to the asymmetry argument is that, taken to its logical conclusion, it would
options: make it impossible to prosecute any whistleblower
justify lower procedural standards for any weaker party — including those making indefensible disclosures
require institutions to publish all internal communications
eliminate the requirement for sincerity as a condition of whistleblowing

questions.prompt: The student's response to the professor's counter-argument is to propose combining the asymmetry argument with
options: stronger legal penalties for institutions found to have engaged in wrongdoing
international whistleblowing standards overseen by a neutral body
substantive conditions — epistemic care, sincerity, and proportionality still apply
mandatory mediation between whistleblower and institution before disclosure

questions.prompt: The additional condition the student proposes is that the wrongdoing must
options: violate national law as defined by a court
involve direct harm to identifiable individuals
have occurred over an extended period with documented evidence
constitute a genuine betrayal of the institution's own stated values and purposes

questions.prompt: The student says this additional condition gives the asymmetry argument 'more traction without making it infinitely expandable.' This means it
options: increases the persuasive force of the argument while limiting who can legitimately claim it
makes the argument legally enforceable in court
allows the argument to be used in more cases than before
gives the argument more popular appeal while reducing its academic precision

questions.prompt: What is the difference between 'procedural' and 'substantive' conditions for whistleblowing?
options: Procedural conditions relate to legal compliance; substantive conditions relate to ethical compliance
Procedural conditions concern how the disclosure is made; substantive conditions concern the severity of the wrongdoing
Procedural conditions apply before disclosure; substantive conditions apply after disclosure
Procedural conditions are set by law; substantive conditions are set by professional ethics codes

cloze.template: The professor presents a case of an employee at a defence {{b1}} who discloses falsified safety reports to a journalist rather than a regulator. The student argues this satisfies the standard conditions for admirable whistleblowing: serious harm, {{b2}} of internal channels, and proportionate disclosure. The professor pushes back on the {{b3}} condition: the disclosure may expose classified technical specifications that could themselves cause harm. The student distinguishes a {{b4}} view — the whistleblower bears responsibility for foreseeable consequences — from a {{b5}} view, which assesses moral worth at the point of decision. The professor uses the {{b6}} imperative to argue that universalising the whistleblower's maxim would collapse the distinction between legitimate disclosure and mere data {{b7}}. The student acknowledges that the relevant procedural conditions are easier to apply {{b8}} than in advance. The professor then draws a parallel with civil {{b9}}: both involve claiming moral authority that democratic procedures distribute more broadly. The student introduces the {{b10}} asymmetry: whistleblowers face organisations with vastly greater legal, financial, and reputational resources, while their own legal protections are {{b11}} and often litigated away. As a further limiting condition, the student proposes that the wrongdoing must constitute a genuine {{b12}} of the institution's own stated values.

summary: A Seminar on the Ethics of Whistleblowing — a C1-level listening lesson.
translationVi: A Seminar on the Ethics of Whistleblowing: A Seminar on the Ethics of Whistleblowing — a C1-level listening lesson.
criticalThinkingQuestion: Based on the arguments presented in this lesson, what do you think is the most important implication for how we think about this topic? Are there perspectives or counterarguments that you feel were not adequately addressed?
